cards.q: Define neonatal jaundice.
cards.a: The yellowish discoloration of the eyes, skin and mucous membranes in the first month of life, due to an elevated level of bilirubin in the blood.

cards.q: Apart from yellow eyes and skin, what other symptoms may neonatal jaundice present with?
cards.a: Yellowing of the palms; dark, yellow urine (a newborn's urine should be colourless); and pale-coloured stool (should be yellow or orange).

cards.q: Elevation of which bilirubin fraction may cause the yellow discoloration?
cards.a: Either the unconjugated or the conjugated fraction.

cards.q: What possible physiologic role may bilirubin have?
cards.a: It may act as an antioxidant.

cards.q: Which bilirubin fraction is potentially neurotoxic?
cards.a: Indirect, unconjugated bilirubin.

cards.q: Is conjugated bilirubin neurotoxic, and what does a raised direct bilirubin indicate?
cards.a: It is not neurotoxic, but direct hyperbilirubinaemia indicates a potentially serious hepatic disorder or a systemic illness.

cards.q: What can poor management of neonatal jaundice result in?
cards.a: Either death or serious neurological problems.

cards.q: How long ago was neonatal jaundice first described in a child textbook?
cards.a: 1000 years ago.

cards.q: Who first described yellow staining of the brain, in what year, and who named the pattern kernicterus?
cards.a: Orth first described it in 1875; the pattern was later referred to by Schmorl as kernicterus.

cards.q: What unconjugated bilirubin level does almost every newborn develop in the first week of life?
cards.a: More than 30 µmol/L (1.8 mg/dL).

cards.q: How does the incidence of neonatal jaundice vary with ethnicity?
cards.a: Higher in East Asians and American Indians, and lower in Africans.

cards.q: How does altitude affect the incidence of neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: Incidence is higher in populations living at high altitudes.

cards.q: Which sex is at higher risk of developing significant neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: Male infants.

cards.q: What is the estimated prevalence of neonatal jaundice in hospital practice, and how does it differ between preterm and term infants?
cards.a: 50–80% overall; about 80% in preterm and about 50% in term infants.

cards.q: What are the two broad categories of the cause of neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: Physiologic and pathologic.

cards.q: Give the first cause of physiological jaundice relating to red cells.
cards.a: Decreased RBC survival (less than 90 days), increased RBC volume per kg, and polycythaemia of the newborn.

cards.q: Give the second cause of physiological jaundice, relating to the liver.
cards.a: Poor hepatic uptake due to an immature liver — decreased ligandin or Y-protein.

cards.q: Give the third cause of physiological jaundice, and its three components.
cards.a: Increased enterohepatic circulation, due to a high level of intestinal beta-glucuronidase, delayed colonisation by bacteria, and decreased gut motility.

cards.q: How is pathological jaundice classified?
cards.a: Into unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia (haemolytic or non-haemolytic) and conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia (hepatic or post-hepatic).

cards.q: Name the Coombs test POSITIVE haemolytic causes of unconjugated pathological jaundice.
cards.a: ABO incompatibility and Rh incompatibility.

cards.q: Name the Coombs test NEGATIVE haemolytic causes of unconjugated pathological jaundice.
cards.a: RBC membrane defects (spherocytosis, elliptocytosis), RBC enzyme defects (G6PD), haemoglobinopathies, sepsis, DIC, haematomas and polycythaemia.

cards.q: Which drugs are listed as a haemolytic cause of neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: Diazepam and oxytocin.

cards.q: List the non-haemolytic causes of unconjugated pathological jaundice.
cards.a: Breast milk jaundice, Crigler–Najjar syndrome types I and II, Gilbert syndrome, and hypothyroidism.

cards.q: List the HEPATIC causes of conjugated pathological jaundice.
cards.a: Idiopathic neonatal hepatitis, Dubin–Johnson syndrome, Rotor's syndrome, infections (TORCH, sepsis), and inborn errors of metabolism (galactosaemia, tyrosinaemia).

cards.q: List the POST-HEPATIC causes of conjugated pathological jaundice.
cards.a: Biliary atresia, bile duct stenosis, and choledochal cyst.

cards.q: List the common causes of neonatal jaundice in our environment.
cards.a: Physiological, blood group incompatibility, G6PD deficiency, breast milk jaundice, sepsis, and cephalhaematoma.

cards.q: List the clinical presentations of neonatal jaundice.
cards.a: Yellowness of the eyes or body, fever, vomiting, inability to suck, features of failure to thrive, abdominal distension, and passage of pale stool.

cards.q: What should be asked about the jaundice itself in the history?
cards.a: Onset, duration and progression, and the colour of the urine and stool.

cards.q: What items of maternal history are relevant in neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: Antenatal history — booking, illness and infections; drugs — sulfa, NSAIDs, herbal; and medical history — blood group, haemolytic diseases and liver disease.

cards.q: What features of the birth history are relevant?
cards.a: Birth trauma and delayed cord clamping.

cards.q: What other history items should be covered?
cards.a: Biodata, vomiting, feeding history and loss of weight.

cards.q: What are the two colour patterns of jaundice on examination, and what does each suggest?
cards.a: Bright yellow or orange, and greenish or muddy yellow.

cards.q: Describe the direction in which jaundice progresses in the newborn.
cards.a: Cephalocaudal — starting on the face and progressing to the abdomen and then the feet as serum levels increase.

cards.q: Kramer's index zone 1 — area and bilirubin level.
cards.a: Face — 4–6 mg/dL.

cards.q: Kramer's index zone 2 — area and bilirubin level.
cards.a: Chest and upper trunk — 8–10 mg/dL.

cards.q: Kramer's index zone 3 — area and bilirubin level.
cards.a: Lower abdomen and thigh — 12–14 mg/dL.

cards.q: Kramer's index zone 4 — area and bilirubin level.
cards.a: Forearms and lower legs — 15–18 mg/dL.

cards.q: Kramer's index zone 5 — area and bilirubin level.
cards.a: Palms and soles — greater than 15–20 mg/dL.

cards.q: Apart from jaundice, what should be looked for on examination?
cards.a: Pallor, dehydration, weight loss, fever or hypothermia, hepato/splenomegaly, evidence of birth trauma or injuries, and evidence of kernicterus.

cards.q: What are the two methods of bilirubin measurement?
cards.a: Serum bilirubin (total and direct), and transcutaneous measurement using a bilirubinometer or icterometer.

cards.q: Which haematological investigations are done in neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: Full blood count and blood smear, haemoglobin concentration, reticulocyte count, blood group and Coombs test.

cards.q: Which urine investigations are done?
cards.a: Urinalysis for reducing substances, and urine microscopy, culture and sensitivity.

cards.q: Which biochemical and other investigations are done?
cards.a: Electrolytes, urea and creatinine; liver function tests (AST/ALT, ALP/GGT); G6PD assay; ultrasound scan; thyroid scan and thyroid function tests.

cards.q: Which babies aged 38 weeks or more are eligible for transcutaneous bilirubin estimation?
cards.a: Those visibly jaundiced after 24 hours of age, until 14 days.

cards.q: Which babies of 34–37+6 weeks gestation are eligible for TcB?
cards.a: Those who are 72 hours old or more, until 14 days.

cards.q: What is the third eligibility criterion for TcB?
cards.a: The baby must not be on phototherapy.

cards.q: List the three contraindications to transcutaneous bilirubin measurement.
cards.a: Babies with pathological jaundice; babies less than 24 hours old; and babies on phototherapy or who have undergone phototherapy or exchange transfusion.

cards.q: What proportion of breastfed infants get breast milk jaundice, and what proportion get breastfeeding jaundice?
cards.a: Breast milk jaundice 2%; breastfeeding jaundice 13%.

cards.q: When does breast milk jaundice occur and when does it peak?
cards.a: After the 7th day, peaking at 2–3 weeks.

cards.q: When does breastfeeding jaundice occur?
cards.a: Within 1 week.

cards.q: What maximum bilirubin levels are reached in breast milk jaundice and in breastfeeding jaundice?
cards.a: Breast milk jaundice may reach 10–30 mg/dL; breastfeeding jaundice may reach more than 12 mg/dL.

cards.q: What happens to the serum bilirubin in breast milk jaundice if breastfeeding stops, and what does it respond to?
cards.a: The serum bilirubin falls gradually if breastfeeding stops; it responds to phototherapy.

cards.q: What does breastfeeding jaundice respond to?
cards.a: Frequent breastfeeding.

cards.q: What is the aetiology of breast milk jaundice?
cards.a: Not clear; it is associated with glucuronidase in the milk.

cards.q: What causes breastfeeding jaundice?
cards.a: Decreased milk intake, dehydration, reduced caloric intake, and glucose water supplementation.

cards.q: What are the two aims of treating unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia?
cards.a: To reduce the incidence of severe hyperbilirubinaemia, and to prevent bilirubin encephalopathy (kernicterus).

cards.q: What are the three treatment modalities for unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia?
cards.a: Phototherapy, exchange blood transfusion, and pharmacotherapy.

cards.q: Define phototherapy.
cards.a: The use of high intensity light energy to reduce bilirubin levels on the skin surface.

cards.q: Which light wavelengths/colours are used in phototherapy, and where does bilirubin absorb maximally?
cards.a: White, blue, turquoise and green light is used; bilirubin absorbs light maximally in the blue range, 420–470 nm.

cards.q: By how much should adequately delivered phototherapy lower bilirubin, and over what period?
cards.a: By 1–2 mg/dL over 4–6 hours.

cards.q: What is the overall mechanism of action of phototherapy?
cards.a: Conversion of insoluble bilirubin into soluble bilirubin.

cards.q: What is structural isomerization in phototherapy?
cards.a: Conversion of bilirubin to lumirubin, which is rapidly excreted in bile and urine.

cards.q: What is photoisomerisation in phototherapy?
cards.a: Conversion of bilirubin (4Z,15Z) to soluble isomers such as 4Z,15E without conjugation, which are excreted into bile.

cards.q: Name the third mechanism of phototherapy.
cards.a: Photooxidation.

cards.q: State the bilirubin thresholds that indicate phototherapy.
cards.a: TSB above 15 mg/dL in term; above 12 mg/dL in preterm; and above 5 mg/dL within 24 hours.

cards.q: Apart from bilirubin thresholds, what are the other indications for phototherapy?
cards.a: As an adjuvant to exchange transfusion; and prophylactic phototherapy in ELBW infants, bruised babies, haemolytic disease of the newborn, and VLBW infants with perinatal risk factors.

cards.q: List the contraindications to phototherapy.
cards.a: Porphyria; conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia (causes bronze baby syndrome); unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia of 20 mg/dL or more; and diseases with hypersensitivity to light.

cards.q: What distance should the phototherapy unit be from the skin, in standard and in intensive phototherapy?
cards.a: 45 cm for standard phototherapy, and 15–20 cm for intensive phototherapy.

cards.q: What space should be left between the phototherapy unit and the incubator?
How can double surface phototherapy be delivered?
cards.a: By fibre-optic blankets (biliblankets).

cards.q: Which parts of the baby must be covered during phototherapy?
cards.a: The eyes and the genitals.

cards.q: How often should the baby's position be changed during phototherapy, and how often should the bilirubin level be checked?
cards.a: Position changed every 2–4 hours; level checked every 10–20 hours.

cards.q: What supplemental hydration is given during phototherapy?
cards.a: 10–25 mL/kg/day.

cards.q: What other monitoring is required during phototherapy?
cards.a: Frequent temperature monitoring, daily weight check, distancing the child from others not needing phototherapy, and watching for side effects.

cards.q: List the EARLY complications of phototherapy.
cards.a: Loose stools, dehydration, skin rashes, hyperpigmentation, upset of maternal–infant interaction (bonding), hyperthermia, and bronze baby syndrome.

cards.q: List the LATE complications of phototherapy.
cards.a: Skin malignancy, damage to DNA, patent ductus arteriosus, gonadal damage, retinal damage, and disturbance of circadian rhythm.

cards.q: What is the purpose of exchange blood transfusion?
cards.a: To rapidly lower the serum bilirubin concentration and/or prevent a further rise in serum bilirubin.

cards.q: State the bilirubin level indications for exchange transfusion in term and in LBW preterm infants.
cards.a: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia of 20 mg/dL and above in term, or 15 mg/dL and above in LBW preterm infants.

cards.q: What is the exchange transfusion threshold for other preterm infants?
cards.a: A serum bilirubin up to 10 mg/dL.

cards.q: What rate of rise of bilirubin indicates exchange transfusion?
cards.a: A bilirubin level rising rapidly, up to 5 mg/dL per day.

cards.q: What early bilirubin levels indicate exchange transfusion?
cards.a: Serum bilirubin above 10 mg/dL on the first day of life, or 15 mg/dL at 48 hours of life.

cards.q: What clinical and other indications exist for exchange transfusion?
cards.a: Clinical signs of kernicterus; and others such as severe anaemia and DIC.

cards.q: What technique and access is commonly used for exchange transfusion?
cards.a: The 'push–pull' method using an umbilical vein catheter.

cards.q: Which blood is preferred for exchange transfusion, and what alternatives may be used?
cards.a: Heparinised, fresh whole blood is preferred; ACD or CPD blood can also be used.

cards.q: What quantity of blood is exchanged, and over what time?
cards.a: Twice the blood volume of the patient (160–170 mL/kg), over 60–90 minutes.

cards.q: In what aliquots is blood exchanged?
cards.a: In aliquots of 5, 10 or 20 mL, or smaller volumes for small infants.

cards.q: How does priming with albumin increase the efficiency of exchange transfusion, and what dose is used?
cards.a: Infusion of 1 mg/kg of salt-poor albumin a few hours before the exchange increases efficiency.

cards.q: Why is the stomach emptied before exchange transfusion?
cards.a: To prevent aspiration.

cards.q: What measurements are taken before and after exchange transfusion?
cards.a: Pre-EBT and post-EBT packed cell volume and serum bilirubin.

cards.q: How much calcium gluconate is given during exchange transfusion, how often, and with what precaution?
cards.a: 1 mL of 10% calcium gluconate after every 100 mL, given slowly while listening to the heart alongside.

cards.q: Why must drugs be replaced after exchange transfusion?
cards.a: All drugs given earlier must be replaced if it is not yet time for the next dose, since they were removed with the exchanged blood.

cards.q: How is hypoglycaemia prevented after exchange transfusion?
cards.a: With 200 mg/kg of IV glucose.

cards.q: What should follow an exchange transfusion?
cards.a: Adjuvant phototherapy.

cards.q: List the complications of catheterisation during exchange transfusion.
cards.a: Gut perforation and peritonitis; wrong position of the catheter tip (which may excite cardiac arrhythmia if in the right atrium); necrotising enterocolitis; and liver cirrhosis as a long-term complication.

cards.q: List the complications of blood transfusion during exchange transfusion.
cards.a: Transmission of infection (malaria, hepatitis B, HIV); transfusion reactions, immediate and delayed; and thromboembolism.

cards.q: List the complications of the exchange process itself.
cards.a: Haemodynamic changes from the push–pull process (hypovolaemia, hypervolaemia); electrolyte changes (hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia, hypomagnesaemia); acid/base imbalance; and changes in cerebral blood flow.

cards.q: Why is pharmacotherapy not routine in neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: Because of its ineffectiveness.

cards.q: What is the mechanism of action of phenobarbitone in neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: It augments hepatic uptake of bilirubin and increases the activity of glucuronyl transferase (UDPG-T) enzyme.

cards.q: How long does phenobarbitone take to reach therapeutic level, what is the dose, and what is its main side effect?
cards.a: 48–72 hours; dose 5–8 mg/kg/day; it sedates, making the baby difficult to assess.

cards.q: In which condition is phenobarbitone particularly useful?
cards.a: Crigler–Najjar disease.

cards.q: What is the mechanism of metalloprotoporphyrins such as tin mesoporphyrin?
cards.a: They are competitive inhibitors of haem oxygenase; they are experimental but showing prospects.

cards.q: How does oral agar (e.g. cholestyramine) work in neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: It decreases the enterohepatic circulation.

cards.q: How does albumin infusion help in neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: It increases albumin binding to bilirubin for easy transport.

cards.q: What is the indication, mechanism and dose of intravenous immunoglobulin in neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: Used for infants with Rh or ABO isoimmunisation; it inhibits haemolysis; dose 0.5–1 g/kg/dose IV, repeated in 12 hours.

cards.q: Define kernicterus.
cards.a: The pathologic diagnosis referring to the yellow staining of the brain caused by bilirubin deposition, associated with neuronal necrosis and gliosis.

cards.q: Kernicterus is a direct consequence of what?
cards.a: Very high levels of unconjugated bilirubin.

cards.q: Which parts of the brain are commonly affected in kernicterus?
cards.a: Basal ganglia, brain stem nuclei, cerebellar nuclei, hippocampus, and anterior horn cells of the spinal cord.

cards.q: In what proportion of infants does kernicterus develop by pathologic criteria, and under what conditions?
cards.a: In 30% of infants with untreated haemolytic disease and bilirubin levels above 25–30 mg/dL.

cards.q: What is the prognosis once overt neurologic signs of kernicterus appear?
cards.a: Grave — more than 75% of infants die, and 80% of affected survivors have bilateral choreoathetosis with involuntary muscle spasms.

cards.q: Describe phase 1 of acute bilirubin encephalopathy, including its timing.
cards.a: Days 1–3: hypotonia, lethargy, high-pitched cry, poor suck, and loss of the Moro reflex.

cards.q: Describe phase 2 of acute bilirubin encephalopathy, including its timing.
cards.a: Middle of the first week: hypertonia, opisthotonus, rigidity, oculogyric crisis, retrocollis, fever and seizures. Those who survive develop chronic bilirubin encephalopathy.

cards.q: Describe phase 3 of acute bilirubin encephalopathy.
cards.a: After the first week, hypotonia replaces hypertonia.

cards.q: How does chronic bilirubin encephalopathy manifest in the first year?
cards.a: Hypotonia, active deep tendon reflexes, obligatory tonic neck reflexes, and delayed motor skills.

cards.q: How does chronic bilirubin encephalopathy manifest after the first year?
cards.a: Movement disorders (choreoathetosis, ballismus, tremor), upward gaze, and sensorineural hearing loss.

cards.q: What is the treatment of established kernicterus?
cards.a: Counselling and training the parents on coping with the sequelae; management of feeding difficulty; physiotherapy for limitation of disability; speech and language therapy (SALT); and follow-up appointments.

cards.q: What is the general approach to treating conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia?
cards.a: Mainly supportive.

cards.q: Which vitamins are given in conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and why?
cards.a: Parenteral vitamin K; and vitamin A and D supplementation, which prevents rickets.

cards.q: What diet is recommended in conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia?
cards.a: Low fat, with medium chain triglycerides.

cards.q: What is the role of phenobarbitone in conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, and when is surgery needed?
cards.a: Phenobarbitone helps bile excretion; surgery may be needed, but not usually in the neonatal period.

cards.q: What does prevention of neonatal jaundice entail?
cards.a: Prevention of neonatal jaundice, and the treatment and prevention of its complications when it occurs — following the 5 levels of prevention.

cards.q: Level 1 of prevention — general health promotion. What does it involve?
cards.a: Educating the masses about neonatal jaundice and how to prevent it, and lifestyle modification.

cards.q: Level 2 of prevention — specific protection. What does it involve?
cards.a: Identifying risks and instituting preventive measures.

cards.q: Level 3 of prevention. What does it involve?
cards.a: Early diagnosis and adequate treatment — identifying and treating jaundice promptly.

cards.q: Levels 4 and 5 of prevention. What do they involve?
cards.a: Level 4, limitation of disability — physiotherapy. Level 5, rehabilitation — integration of the baby and the mother into the community.

cards.q: What is the conclusion of the lecture regarding the burden of neonatal jaundice?
cards.a: It still contributes significantly to neonatal morbidity, mortality and long-term handicap, especially in developing countries — made worse by ignorance on the part of parents and health workers, which delays proper medical care.

cards.q: What measures does the lecture conclude will prevent jaundice-associated problems?
cards.a: Education at primary health care level, prompt diagnosis, and referral to the appropriate specialist — so that death is averted and the child has a good quality of life.
